item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Presumably, it'd be treated like any other asset and the local intestate succession laws would kick in. In the US it varies by state, but in general it'd go to spouses, children, possibly siblings and/or parents, and in some cases it would 'fall through' dead heirs. If no heirs can be found it becomes property of the state. Any of these would almost certainly result in the reds ending up outside Foundation control, as well as possibly allowing them to find out where the site they were stored in is.

It wouldn't be the worst breach - the courts would have to figure it out and the Foundation could easily track who ended up with it, and get it back either using 2855 or (more likely) simply sending someone pretending to be a numismatist to buy it off them for above face value using normal cash or a check.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I really liked this one, especially since there are actual towns that have their own currency that only works in the town.

But the whole time I kept wondering, what if you gave them all to a D class had him put them in a safe, then terminated him? Presumably all the people of Timothy have had wills so ownership transferred. But if he died without leaving them to anyone would they be safe?

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: As the author, I would be happy with these edits being made to the article.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

This is a pretty self-contained article, but I do see one potential crosslink that can be used. In the event of a hypothetical massive breach of SCP-2566 notes, and I mean massive, I could foresee the Foundation using SCP-2855's ability to help in re-containment efforts. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Due to the anomalous properties of SCP-2566, the long-term storage of SCP-2566 has been deemed inviable as a containment procedure. At the beginning of each month, the Assistant Quartermasters at Site-11 are to distribute instances of SCP-2566 as bonus pay among designated employees permanently stationed at Site-11. Those employees are to store their instances of SCP-2566 in their personal SCP-2566 temporary storage containers. By the end of each month, those employees are to return their instances of SCP-2566 to the Assistant Quartermasters in exchange for products from Site-11's commissary. RFID tags are to be attached to all instances of SCP-2566 to facilitate the tracking of their current location and ownership by the Head Quartermaster.

To this:

Due to the anomalous properties of SCP-2566, the long-term storage of SCP-2566 has been deemed inviable as a containment procedure. At the beginning of each month, the Assistant Quartermasters at Site-11 are to distribute instances of SCP-2566 as bonus pay among designated employees permanently stationed at Site-11. Those employees are to store their instances of SCP-2566 in their personal SCP-2566 temporary storage containers. By the end of each month, those employees are to return their instances of SCP-2566 to the Assistant Quartermasters in exchange for products from Site-11's commissary. RFID tags are to be attached to all instances of SCP-2566 to facilitate the tracking of their current location and ownership by the Head Quartermaster.

In the event of a catastrophic breach of SCP-2566's containment, use of SCP-2855 has been approved for assistance in re-containment efforts.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Anomalous economists/sociologists is not an idea I would have taken seriously if I had heard it before reading this article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I see. My profs tend to speak of many social experiments conducted by the Americans back in the day, although going out abroad would be a suitable adaptation to research ethics in the developed world.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
in the US

Actually, I think that you could use the Society for Alternative Social Analysis in a variety of different settings. Holding social experiments in developing countries and among indigenous peoples is a thing that happens in real life, and I'd imagine that the governments of less developed countries would be more willing to overlook SASA's more ethically dubious experiments given the proper incentives. I think that it would also allow you to explore themes of neocolonialism.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Society for Alternative Social Analysis

Hmm, I will remember this group if I ever have an anomalous social experiment in the US during the 20th century.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I still love it, and I'm intrigued by the Society for Alternative Social Analysis

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really enjoyed this very much when I read it as your 3000 entry, and I felt bad about feeling unable to upvote as while I liked it, I didn't think it fit the contest and would not have liked it introducing Series IV as 3000. So glad to upvote it now on the mainlist, and definitely seconding TL333s on interest in the GoI!

item.title: Re: Thanks, Attributions, and Thoughts
item.content:encoded: Did I just make a terrible pun? I really played myself. ._.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is a much better place for this. Upvoted without hesitation.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh right, originally an entry. Works much better as a standard article, so upvoted, and previous comments about interest in the GoI stand! :)

item.title: Thanks, Attributions, and Thoughts
item.content:encoded:
The image was a modified public-domain image taken from WikiMedia Commons.

My thanks to sirpudding, who reviewed this as a draft on the forums, and shaggydreadlocks, who reviewed this on the chat!

This article was initially posted as an SCP-3000 contest entry, but I've decided to retract it and post it as a mainlist article for Series III. While I would have liked to get this into Series IV through the contest, I admit that it doesn't quite fit the bill for horror, which isn't really my forte at all. So, rather than trying to shoehorn this into the SCP-3000 contest, I will let it be judged in its own terms.
